text: Last class we made our case and emphasized exactly what mattered.
Today the whole room is in the meeting, and the real message is often unspoken.

Never have I seen such pushback in a meeting.
Not only did we slip, but we also lost trust.
Rarely does engineering push back this hard.
Only after the demo did they finally agree.
Before we start, let us name the elephant in the room. Not only is the launch slipping, but trust is slipping too. I will not sugarcoat that.
Fair. Rarely does engineering admit this, but there was an undercurrent of frustration. We felt rushed, so the team kept hedging.
Thank you for saying it. Never have I seen a clearer subtext: the team needs time. So let us not downplay the real risk.
Agreed, with one caveat: only when testing is done will we commit to a date. I do not want to gloss over the gaps.
Good. That is the nuance I needed. So our tacit rule is honest numbers, no sugarcoating, and a firm date once testing closes.
Done. Not only did we name the problem, but we also fixed the pushback. Little did I expect such an honest meeting.

Hi Fabiana, it is Klaus. Before the cross-functional meeting this afternoon, let me give you a quick heads-up, with one caveat: this stays between us. Never have I seen so much unspoken tension between product and operations. Not only is the launch slipping, but the team is also losing trust. Rarely does engineering push back this hard, so read between the lines when they speak. The subtext is simple: they feel rushed and ignored. Only when someone names the elephant in the room will we move forward. So please do not gloss over the delay, and do not sugarcoat the numbers. A little understatement is fine, but the tacit deal is that we stay honest with each other. Little did I expect this much friction, yet here we are. Talk soon.

Hi team, here is a quick read on the cross-functional meeting. It went better than the subtext suggested. Not only did operations raise the delay, but they also named the real concern out loud. Never have I seen the room shift so fast once the elephant in the room was on the table. There was pushback at first, and engineering tried to downplay the risk, but I did not let them gloss over it. Only after we admitted the slip did the tone finally change. The understatement of the day was a minor reschedule, when in fact it is a three-week delay. Still, the tacit agreement now is clear: no more hedging, and no more sugarcoating. Read between the lines and you will see real support for the new plan. Little did they expect me to push so hard, but it paid off. I will send the notes shortly.

Klaus leaves a voicemail warning Fabiana about the unspoken tension before the meeting. In the room, operations finally names the elephant in the room. Engineering pushes back and tries to downplay the delay. Fabiana refuses to gloss over it and gets the team to admit the slip. The meeting ends with a tacit agreement to stay honest and stop sugarcoating.
